shower.thought: We're all living in the past because it takes time for light to reach our eyes—you've never actually seen the present moment.
The worst part about growing up isn't losing your imagination; it's knowing exactly how limited it actually was.
You can't remember most of your life, which means you've already forgotten being the person you were trying to become.
Every apology you've accepted from someone else is a version of you deciding to stop being angry at a person who no longer exists.
The people who need therapy the most are usually the ones most convinced they don't.
You're only as free as the things you're willing to be bored by.
Nostalgia is just grief wearing a prettier mask.
The moment you understand why someone did something terrible, you can never fully hate them again.
We teach children to be honest, then spend their entire lives punishing them for it.
Your body is constantly replacing itself, so the "you" from seven years ago is literally dead—you're mourning a stranger.
The scariest thing about getting older isn't dying, it's realizing how many people have already stopped trying to understand you.
You've probably met the person you were meant to spend your life with and had no idea, which is somehow more painful than never meeting them.
We document our entire lives now, but we're probably forgetting how to actually experience them.
The only real difference between confidence and delusion is whether things work out.
You can't prove anyone else is conscious, which means you're living in a world that might be completely empty except for you.
Time moves faster the older you get because each year becomes a smaller percentage of your life—but only if you're not paying attention.
Some of your best ideas came to you while you were doing nothing, which means productivity culture might be the enemy of actual creation.
We're terrified of being alone until we realize that's where all the thinking happens.
The people who hurt you most are usually the ones who saw you most clearly and decided it wasn't enough.
You spend decades building a version of yourself for other people, then die before you can finally be yourself for anyone.

motivational.thought: The only way to do great work is to love what you're doing. — Steve Jobs
Your limitation—it's only your lack of vision. — Unknown
Progress is progress, no matter how small. — Unknown
Discipline is choosing between what you want now and what you want most. — Unknown
The man who moves a mountain begins by carrying away small stones. — Confucius
You don't rise to the level of your goals; you fall to the level of your systems. — James Clear
What we fear doing most is usually what we most need to do. — Tim Ferriss
Become obsessed with your solution, not your problem. — Unknown
The best time to plant a tree was 20 years ago. The second best time is now. — Chinese Proverb
Mediocrity is a choice. Excellence is a habit. — Unknown
Your future self is watching you right now through memories yet to be made. — Unknown
The cave you fear to enter holds the treasure you seek. — Joseph Campbell
You're not too old, you're not too late, and you're not too broken. — Unknown
Small daily improvements are the antidote to comparison. — Unknown
Comfort is the enemy of growth. — Neale Donald Walsch
The person you'll be in five years is determined by the books you read and the people you surround yourself with today. — Unknown
Don't wait for the perfect moment. Take the moment and make it perfect. — Unknown
Your scars are proof that you're stronger than what tried to break you. — Unknown
The gap between who you are and who you want to be? That's called growth. — Unknown
Regret weighs more than effort ever will. — Unknown

darkjoke.thought:
My wife said she's leaving me for someone who actually listens to her.
I wasn't really paying attention, but I hope it works out for her.

I told my psychiatrist I had suicidal thoughts.
He said that'll be $200, and booked me for next month.

My dad said I'd never amount to anything.
He was right, but I appreciated the motivation.

I went to a funeral for someone I didn't know.
The open bar made it feel less like a waste of time.

My therapist asked if I was having thoughts of self-harm.
I said only on Mondays, Tuesdays, Wednesdays, Thursdays, and Fridays.
She seemed relieved I had weekends off.

I asked my friend why he never visits anymore.
He said he didn't realize I was still alive.

My doctor told me I need to cut back on drinking.
I told him I'd get a second opinion.
He said that's the alcohol talking.
I said exactly, and it has better ideas than he does.

I'm not clinically depressed, I'm just realistic.
My therapist said those are the same thing.
I haven't been back since.

My mom asked why I don't call anymore.
I said I assumed you'd heard the news.
She still doesn't get that I'm joking.

I went to a support group for people with dark humor.
Turns out we weren't actually allowed to joke about anything.
I've never felt more supported.

My ex said I have commitment issues.
She's right—I couldn't commit to staying in that relationship.

I told my landlord about the mice in my apartment.
He said they pay rent more reliably than I do.

My therapist said I'm avoidant.
I agreed and then switched therapists.

I got rejected from a dating app.
The algorithm knows me better than I know myself.

My friend said I'm selfish.
I told him to stop making everything about him.

I asked my barber why he kept cutting my hair shorter each time.
He said eventually I'd just stop coming.
Finally, a long-term plan.

My dad said he was disappointed in me.
I said, "Get in line, buddy."
He said, "I have been. For 30 years."

I told my doctor I had trouble sleeping.
He said that's normal after what I've been through.
I said, "We've never met before."
He said, "Exactly."

My accountant asked why I had so many deductions for "personal catastrophes."
I said they're tax-deductible life choices.

I went to a couples therapist alone.
The therapist asked where my partner was.
I said, "That's what we're here to figure out."
